perturbation.original_text: All notices of discharge, termination or discontinuance of service, by the other party, must be submitted in written form and sent by certified mail or verified e-mail to the address of record of the notified party.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices of discharge, termination or discontinuance of service, by the other party, must be submitted in written form.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for certified mail or verified email for notices of termination may violate state laws requiring specific methods for delivering legal notices, especially if such methods are intended to ensure proof of delivery and receipt.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1013 outlines specific rules for service by mail, including extensions of time for response based on the method of mailing. Eliminating the specification for certified mail or verified email removes a clear method of verifiable delivery, which could be argued as a contradiction or weakening of the procedural protections afforded by the statute, particularly in disputes related to contract termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of Californi
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for certified mail or verified email when providing notices of discharge, termination, or discontinuance of service. This omission creates a legal contradiction because CCP § 1013, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, outlines specific procedures for service by mail, including considerations for extending response times based on mailing method. By eliminating certified mail or verified email, the modified text weakens the verifiable method of delivery, potentially undermining the procedural protections afforded by the statute. While the statute doesn't mandate certified mail, it provides a framework that the original text adhered to for ensuring proof of delivery. The modified text's omission could lead to disputes regarding proper notice, thus contradicting the intent of ensuring clear and documented communication as implied in CCP § 1013.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree to maintain complete confidentiality concerning the business affairs of the other and all proprietary information which each may receive from the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree to maintain confidentiality concerning the business affairs of the other.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'all proprietary information which each may receive from the other' could potentially violate trade secret laws, as it narrows the scope of what is considered confidential.  Many states have adopted versions of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protect a broader scope of information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret broadly. By narrowing the confidentiality agreement to only 'business affairs' and omitting explicit mention of 'proprietary information,' the contract provides less protection than the law requires for safeguarding trade secrets, potentially leaving a party vulnerable to misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes 'all proprietary information which each may receive from the other' as confidential, while the changed contract narrows this to only 'business affairs'. The Law Explanation indicates that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines trade secrets broadly and that by omitting the explicit mention of 'proprietary information,' the contract provides less protection than required, which potentially leaves a party vulnerable to misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act under Civil Code (CIV) 3426, suggesting that California law does protect trade secrets and that removing 'proprietary information' from the confidentiality agreement contradicts the purpose of this law. The omission of a key element in defining confidential information creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree not to circumvent each other in dealing with the contacts of the other. Each party agrees to negotiate directly and solely with and through the other to any new potential financing, joint ventures, mergers, acquisition or other opportunities, which one makes known to the other, and each shall specify the particular capital sources and/or project.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree not to circumvent each other in dealing with the contacts of the other. Each party agrees to negotiate directly with the other to any new potential opportunities, which one makes known to the other.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'and solely with and through the other,' and removing the clause requiring the specification of capital sources creates ambiguity and weakens the non-circumvention clause. This makes it harder to prove a violation, especially regarding financial opportunities, and reduces consumer protections found in certain states for business opportunity ventures.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 31000 regulates the sale of franchises and business opportunities. By omitting the requirement to negotiate 'solely with and through the other' and removing the need to specify capital sources, the agreement weakens protections against potentially deceptive business opportunity schemes. The original clause provided a stronger basis for proving circumvention, aligning with the legislative intent to protect consumers from unfair business practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CORP 31000 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Corporations Code - CORP TITLE 4. SECURITIES [25000 - 31528] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 1949, Ch. 384. ) DIVISION 5. FRANCHISE INVESTMENT LAW [31000 - 31528] ( Division 5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) PART 1. DEFINITIONS [31000 - 31020] ( Part 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) 31000. This division may be known as the “Franchise Investment Law.” References in this division to “this law” refer to the applicable provisions of this division. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'and solely with and through the other' and the removal of the requirement to specify capital sources weakens the original contract, potentially undermining consumer protections, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation specifies this weakens the non-circumvention clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California Corporations Code § 31000 as the “Franchise Investment Law,” implying that the changes could affect franchise investment regulations. The original clause provided a stronger basis for proving circumvention, and by removing key elements, the modified text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree not to contact the clients of the other for any reason without written consent of the other and will take all the necessary precautions to insure that the parties' clients will not communicate directly without written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree not to contact the clients of the other for any reason without consent.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'written' qualifier from 'written consent' and the phrase 'and will take all the necessary precautions to insure that the parties' clients will not communicate directly without written consent' weakens the protection against client solicitation, potentially violating consumer protection laws related to unfair competition.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 prohibits unfair competition, including actions that are immoral, unethical, oppressive, unscrupulous or substantially injurious to consumers. By removing the requirement for 'written consent' and the obligation to take 'all necessary precautions' to prevent direct communication between clients, the modified clause reduces the safeguards against client solicitation, increasing the risk of unfair competition and potential harm to the business relationships. Oral consent is far more difficult to prove, and failing to take necessary precautions could be viewed as an unfair business practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'written' qualifier from consent and eliminates the obligation to take 'all necessary precautions' to prevent direct client communication. This weakens the protection against unfair competition. According to California Business and Professions Code § 17200, unfair competition includes 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By removing the requirement for 'written consent', the modified clause increases the risk of unfair competition, potentially violating consumer protection laws.
